worth_it_explanation: Names are the spine of every filter, pivot, and rollup the team builds, so a name that omits the dimensions the account reports on forces those dimensions to be rebuilt by hand each time. The check is quick. Campaign Manager lists the campaign groups, campaigns, and ad sets in one view, so a scan of the names against a documented pattern shows the gaps fast. The fix is a rename plus a written convention going forward, not a rebuild, and it pays off on every report after that.
intro: Naming conventions govern how campaign groups, campaigns, and ad sets are labeled across a LinkedIn Ads account, and a consistent pattern lets the names carry the reporting metadata the team filters and rolls up on, such as objective, audience, and creative variant. LinkedIn caps a campaign name at 50 characters, so the convention has to encode those dimensions in a compact, agreed shorthand. These checks confirm that a documented convention exists and is applied, and that the names actually encode the dimensions the account reports on.

audit_checks.title: There is no consistent naming convention across campaigns
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Without a shared naming pattern, filtering and rollup reporting break down, and two people name the same thing differently. The team then spends time reconciling labels by hand instead of reading performance, and a mislabeled campaign can be paused or scaled by mistake.

educate.base:
A naming convention is a documented pattern for labeling campaign groups, campaigns, and ad sets so that every name follows the same format. In Campaign Manager the name is the handle the team uses to find, filter, and report on a campaign, so the value of a convention is that anyone can read a name and know what it points to without opening it.

When no convention exists, names accumulate in whatever style each person reached for at the time. One campaign reads 'Q1 ABM push', another reads 'webinar_lookalike_v2', and a third reads 'New Campaign (1)'. Filtering by a keyword misses the campaigns that spelled it differently, and a rollup across campaigns has to be assembled by hand because the names do not share a structure to group on.

The 50-character limit on a campaign name shapes the convention. The pattern has to encode the dimensions the team reports on in a compact, agreed shorthand, and it has to be written down so a new team member applies it the same way. A convention that lives only in one person's head drifts the moment someone else creates a campaign.

A few signs point to a missing or unenforced convention:
  • Two campaigns that serve the same objective or audience are named in different styles
  • Names mix separators, casing, and abbreviations with no documented rule
  • Default labels such as 'New Campaign' or 'Copy of...' remain in the account
You may want to confirm whether a written convention exists before judging the names. A team can be consistent by habit, and a team can have a document that no one follows. The audit records both the document and the practice.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a considered or B2B store running on LinkedIn, the convention may want to encode the product line or catalog segment alongside the audience because a store often runs the same offer to several audiences. You may want to confirm the names let the team roll up by product line and by audience separately.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-capture account, which is the common LinkedIn case, the convention may want to encode the offer or asset, such as a specific guide or webinar, so lead volume can be read per asset. You may want to confirm the names tie each campaign to the asset it promotes.

investigate.intro: Confirm whether a written naming convention exists and whether the campaign groups, campaigns, and ad sets in Campaign Manager actually follow it.

linkedin_campaign_manager.label: LinkedIn Campaign Manager
steps: Open Campaign Manager and select the ad account you are auditing from the account dropdown.
Open the 'Campaign groups' tab to read the group names, then open a group and read the campaign names within it, and open a campaign to read its ad set names.

Scan the names for a shared structure. Note where casing, separators, abbreviations, or the order of dimensions differ between items that serve the same purpose, and note any default labels such as 'New Campaign' or 'Copy of...' left in place.
Ask the team for the documented convention, if one exists, and compare the live names against it rather than against a guess at the intended pattern.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether a written naming convention exists and, sampling across campaign groups, campaigns, and ad sets, whether the live names follow it. An account with a documented convention and names that match it passes. An account with no documented convention, or with names that diverge in style across items serving the same purpose, is a fail recorded with examples of the divergent names.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether a written convention exists and note a sample of campaign group, campaign, and ad set names above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s account has no consistent naming convention across its campaign groups, campaigns, and ad sets. The names follow different styles for items that serve the same objective or audience, so filtering misses campaigns that were labeled differently and any rollup across campaigns has to be assembled by hand. {{voice_recommend}} adopting a single documented convention, applying it across the account through a rename pass, and sharing it with everyone who creates campaigns so new work follows the same pattern. Because a campaign name is capped at 50 characters, the convention should encode the key reporting dimensions in a compact, agreed shorthand.

audit_checks.title: Campaign names do not encode objective, audience, or test variant
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A name that omits the dimensions the team reports on, such as objective, audience, and creative variant, forces those dimensions to be reconstructed by hand for every report. Comparisons across audiences or test variants then depend on memory rather than the label, which slows reporting and lets the wrong variant be scaled.

educate.base:
Even with a convention in place, a name only helps the team if it encodes the dimensions the account is reported on. On LinkedIn the dimensions that usually matter are the objective, the audience or segment, and the creative or test variant. Those are what the team compares performance across. A name that carries them lets a person read the label and know what the campaign is testing without opening it.

When the metadata is missing, the dimensions still exist in the account, but they live in settings rather than in the name. A report that wants to compare two audiences against the same offer, or variant A against variant B, has to open each campaign to recover which is which. The name does the work of a filter only when it holds the values worth filtering on.

The 50-character limit means a name cannot spell every dimension out in full, so the convention encodes them as a short, agreed code, for example an objective abbreviation, an audience tag, and a variant marker in a fixed order. The order matters because a consistent order is what lets the team group on the same position every time.

You may want to confirm which dimensions the team actually reports on before judging the names, then check whether the names carry those specific dimensions. A name can look detailed and still omit the one field the reporting depends on, for example a variant marker on an A/B test where the two ad sets are otherwise identical.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a considered or B2B store, the metadata worth encoding often includes the product line alongside the audience and objective because the same audience may see different product offers. You may want to confirm the names let a report separate performance by product line, not only by audience.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-capture account, the dominant LinkedIn case, the asset or offer is usually the dimension reporting hinges on, so the name may want to encode the specific guide, webinar, or form alongside the audience. You may want to confirm the names tie lead volume to the asset that drove it.

learn_more.source_title: Understanding ad set settings in Campaign Manager

investigate.intro: Confirm which reporting dimensions the team uses, then check whether the campaign and ad set names actually encode the objective, audience, and creative or test variant.

linkedin_campaign_manager.label: LinkedIn Campaign Manager
steps: Open Campaign Manager, select the ad account, and open the 'Campaigns' tab so the campaign names are listed alongside their objective column.
For a sample of campaigns, read the name and then open the campaign and its ad set settings to see the real objective, audience, and creative in use.
Compare what the settings say against what the name encodes. Note any campaign whose name omits the objective, the audience or segment, or the test variant that the team reports on.
For any A/B test, confirm the two variants are distinguishable by name alone rather than only by opening each ad set.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for a sample of campaigns, whether the name encodes the objective, the audience or segment, and the creative or test variant that the team reports on. Names that carry the reported dimensions pass. Names that omit a dimension the reporting depends on, or that leave A/B variants indistinguishable by name, are a fail recorded with the missing dimension and example names.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record which dimensions the team reports on and a sample of campaign names above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s campaign names do not encode the objective, audience, and creative or test variant that the account is reported on. Those dimensions live in campaign settings rather than in the names, so any report that compares audiences or test variants has to open each campaign to recover which is which. {{voice_recommend}} encoding the key reporting dimensions in the names through a short, agreed shorthand in a fixed order, keeping each name within the 50-character limit. With the dimensions in the names, the team can filter and roll up performance directly from the labels rather than reconstructing them by hand.
